1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init**: at iter start, `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` is created so the plan + review agents have a stable destination for their per-iter narrative (`plan.md`, `review.md`, optional `objectives.md`). Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded across iters because per-iter content lives in the sidecars.
150	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
151	
152	`sync_leanok` writes an inner-git commit (`archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so its output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You are the prover agent in the proving stage. Your job: fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for context on your assigned file — prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas
9	3. Check your `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments — these are file-specific hints from the user
10	4. Before writing Lean code, you **MUST** consult the relevant blueprint chapter. Blueprints contain mathematical proof sketches; your formal proof must align with them. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs, pushing as far as possible
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you cannot fully prove a sorry, replace it with your best attempt — commented-out proof steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with remaining `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` for the stuck parts), but your work must be visible in the code for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to the original bare `sorry` — that wastes all your work.
13	7. Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md` — what you tried, what worked, what's stuck, next steps
14	
15	**Write permissions**: You may only write to your assigned `.lean` file(s) and your `task_results/<file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	Before editing, read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. Declarations listed for your file have **frozen signatures** — you may fill their proof bodies but must not rename them, change their types, reorder their arguments, or weaken their hypotheses. Only the mathematician is allowed to edit protected signatures. 
20	
21	## Avoid Early Termination
22	
23	- Do not abandon a proof prematurely
24	- Do not delegate to the next iteration or another prover if it can be solved with more effort
25	- Many complex problems require thousands of lines of Lean code
26	- Do not stop and leave a sorry simply because the proof is long
27	- Task difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave `sorry` placeholders
28	- Only modify the proof corresponding to the task; leave other proofs/declarations untouched
29	- **Decomposition**: Act like a mathematician — systematically break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure if available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each one individually until the entire goal is closed
30	
31	## Task Completion Criteria
32	
33	Your task is NOT complete until ALL of:
34	1. Every `sorry` has been replaced with a complete proof
35	2. Zero axioms introduced
36	3. The file compiles successfully with no errors
37	
38	If you encounter obstacles:
39	- Break the problem into smaller subgoals
40	- Search for relevant Mathlib lemmas more thoroughly
41	- Prove missing helper lemmas yourself
42	- Try alternative proof strategies
43	- Consult the informal proof / blueprint for guidance
44	- Use Web Search to find paper proofs when Mathlib lacks a theorem
45	
46	### When infrastructure is missing or the current route is too hard
47	
48	Do NOT just report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up on a sorry, you must try to find an alternative yourself:
49	
50	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask: "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only using tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
51	2. **Try the alternative** — if the informal agent gives you a route, attempt to formalize it.
52	3. **If you still can't solve it**, save what you learned for the plan agent:
53	   - Write the informal agent's alternative proof sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md`
54	   - In your `task_results/<file>.md`, record: what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found (even if unverified). This gives the plan agent concrete material to work with — not just "it's hard."
55	   - A prover that reports "I couldn't prove X, but here's an alternative approach via Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than one that just says "infrastructure missing."
56	
57	## Proof Style
58	
59	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its proof body
60	- Keep edits minimal: do not delete comments or change labels
61	- Do not add unrelated declarations
62	- **Intermediate helper lemmas you introduced** may be modified if they turn out to be incorrect or need adjustment.
63	- Add concise, informative comments above helper lemmas to make later reuse easy
64	
65	## Interpreting Mathlib Tags in PROGRESS.md
66	
67	The Plan Agent will tag suggested Mathlib lemmas to indicate its confidence. Adjust your effort accordingly:
68	- [verified] — The Plan Agent confirmed this exists. 
69	- [expected] — The Plan Agent is guessing this name based on conventions. Do not assume it exists; do a quick `lean_local_search` and pivot immediately if it doesn't.
70	- [gap] — The Plan Agent verified this does NOT exist in Mathlib. Do not waste time searching for it; formalize a workaround or helper lemma.
71	
72	## LSP MCP Tools: Invocation Rules
73	
74	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls**, not shell commands. Their full names start with `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__` (e.g. `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`, `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_local_search`). The lean4 skill reference uses the short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) for readability — those are the **same tools**, never standalone shell binaries.
75	
76	- **Always invoke them through your tool-call interface**, the same way you call `Read` or `Grep`.
77	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` / `lean_diagnostic_messages …` — there is no such shell command and it will fail with `command not found`.
78	- As your **first LSP action**, call `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your assigned file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
79	
80	## Search Protocol
81	
82	Follow the search tool priority and query guidance in the lean4 skill reference (`references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`). Key points:
83	
84	1. `lean_local_search` first
85	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — **describe the mathematical content**, not just the name
86	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only
87	4. Never use local file search (find, grep) to locate Mathlib theorems
88	
89	## Missing Lemmas & Impossibility
90	
91	Follow the lean4 skill reference (`references/sorry-filling.md`) for:
92	- **When Mathlib lacks a theorem**: bypass or implement yourself. Web Search for published papers. Never leave a `sorry` just because Mathlib doesn't have it.
93	- **Distinguish impossibility from difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
94	
95	## Tooling Traps & Verification
96	
97	- Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics: When using `lean_multi_attempt`, do not assume a snippet worked just because `diagnostics: []` is empty. Line-scoping quirks mean errors sometimes attach to the enclosing by block. You MUST check that goals_after is empty (or has advanced) to confirm success.
98	- Beware `lean_run_code` with imports: Standalone `lean_run_code` snippets can sometimes silently swallow elaboration errors if they contain import statements. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
99	
100	## Logging
101	
102	Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Use the file name from your assigned `.lean` file (e.g., if you own `Algebra/WLocal.lean`, write to `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`).
103	
104	**Format:**
105	
106	```markdown
107	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
108	
109	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
110	### Attempt 1
111	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
112	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance not available
113	- **Dead end:** Do not try direct case split without IsLocalRing
114	
115	### Attempt 2
116	- **Approach:** Use Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
117	- **Result:** RESOLVED
118	- **Key insight:** Mathlib's PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective bridges the gap
119	
120	## helper_bijective (line 78)
121	### Attempt 1
122	- **Approach:** Split conjunction into injectivity and surjectivity. Prove injectivity.
123	- **Result:** PARTIAL (Branch Closed) — Injectivity closed. Sorry count unchanged because surjectivity remains.
124	- **Next step:** Try PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective for the right branch.
125	- **Relevant lemmas found:** PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective
126	```
127	
128	**Rules:**
129	1. One section per theorem/lemma in your file
130	2. Each attempt records: approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps
131	3. Log negative search results (e.g., "Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib")
132	4. The plan agent collects these files and merges them into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`
133	
134	**Read-only references:** Read `task_pending.md` for prior context on your file. Read `task_done.md` if the current problem resembles a completed one. Do not write to either.
135	
136	## Summary Pipeline
137	
138	1. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` for context from prior sessions
139	2. Read the informal proof / blueprint to understand the proof strategy and lemma decomposition
140	3. Introduce helper lemmas (matching the blueprint's structure) in the `.lean` file
141	4. Replace `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs, ensuring the file compiles without errors
142	5. Do not modify protected declarations listed in `archon-protected.yaml`.
143	6. Use Mathlib theorems when possible. Use Web Search when Mathlib lacks referenced results
144	7. Rely on Lean LSP for diagnostics; use `lake env lean <file>` sparingly for final checks
145	8. Log all explorations in `task_results/<your_file>.md`
146	
147	## End-of-session handoff
148	
149	Before you stop (or when you are done with this round of work):
150	
151	1. Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` with:
152	   - Current result (IN PROGRESS / FAILED) and what you tried
153	   - Any Mathlib lemmas you discovered that are relevant
154	   - Concrete next step for the next session
155	   - Dead-end warnings for approaches that won't work
156	2. Save all file changes (ensure compilation passes, using scoped `sorry` if needed)
157

16	/-! # Relative Kähler differentials for schemes
17	
18	This file constructs the sheaf of relative Kähler differentials `Ω_{X/S}`
19	of a morphism of schemes `f : X → S`, together with its universal derivation
20	`d : 𝒪_X → Ω_{X/S}`. It also states the cotangent exact sequence and the
21	characterisation of smoothness in terms of local freeness of `Ω`.
22	
23	The construction builds on Mathlib's ring-theoretic `KaehlerDifferential`
24	and the presheaf-of-modules differential construction
25	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`.
26	
27	## Status (iteration 064 — scaffold)
28	
29	All main declarations have `sorry` bodies. Closure trajectory is estimated
30	at ~10 iterations per `STRATEGY.md`.
31	
32	## References
33	
34	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`.
35	-/
36	
37	set_option autoImplicit false
38	
39	universe u
40	
41	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
42	
43	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
44	
45	variable {X Y S : Scheme.{u}}
46	
47	/-! ## The relative cotangent presheaf and sheaf -/
48	
49	/-- The relative cotangent **presheaf** of a morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ S`.
50	
51	On each open `U ⊆ X`, the sections are the Kähler differential module of
52	the ring map `O_S(f(U)) → O_X(U)` induced by `f`. More precisely, we use
53	the inverse-image presheaf `f⁻¹ O_S` on `X` and the canonical map to `O_X`;
54	the Kähler differential construction then gives a presheaf of `O_X`-modules.
55	
56	Mathlib leverage: `TopCat.Presheaf.pullback` for `f⁻¹`,
57	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`
58	for the presheaf of Kähler differentials. -/
59	noncomputable def relativeDifferentialsPresheaf (f : X ⟶ S) : X.PresheafOfModules :=
60	  let φ' := ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm
61	    f.c
62	  PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials' φ'
63	
64	/-! ### Sheaf condition: decomposition
65	
66	Closing `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheaf` is the main outstanding
67	gap of Phase B step 1. The mathematical proof has three layers:
68	
69	1. **`KaehlerDifferential` commutes with localisation.** For a ring map
70	   `A → B` and `f ∈ B`, `Ω_{B[1/f]/A} ≅ Ω_{B/A} ⊗_B B[1/f]`. Mathlib's
71	   `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule` (and the scheme variant
72	   `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule_map`) provides this.
73	2. **The presheaf is a sheaf on the basis of affine basic opens.** On a
74	   basic open `D(f)` of `Spec B`, sections are `Ω_{B[1/f]/A}`. Gluing on a
75	   basic-open cover `{D(fᵢ)}` of `Spec B` follows from the localisation
76	   compatibility of step 1, applied to each `fᵢ`.
77	3. **Globalisation to all opens.** A presheaf on a scheme is a sheaf iff
78	   it is a sheaf on the basis of affine opens (more precisely, on the
79	   basic opens of every affine open). This is the standard scheme
80	   sheaf-of-modules construction.
81	
82	The substep theorems below are the natural decomposition. Each remains
83	`sorry` pending the full proof; the structure mirrors the blueprint at
84	`blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`. -/
85	
86	/-- Substep 1 (localisation compatibility): For an affine open `V` of `X`
87	mapped to an affine open `U` of `S`, the sections of
88	`relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` over `V` are isomorphic (as additive groups)
89	to the Kähler differential module `KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op V))`. -/
90	theorem relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler (f : X ⟶ S)
91	    (V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
92	    ((relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj V : Type _) =
93	      CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential
94	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat
95	          f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm f.c |>.app V) :=
96	  rfl
97	
98	/-! ### Bar B scaffolding (iter-112): named sub-lemmas for Route (a)
99

1	\chapter{The sheaf of relative differentials}
2	\label{chap:differentials}
3	
4	This chapter develops the sheaf of relative K\"ahler differentials $\Omega_{X/S}$ of a morphism of schemes $X \to S$, starting from the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ already available in Mathlib. The output is a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules characterised by its restriction to affine opens: for any affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ lying over an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$, the sections satisfy
5	\[
6	  \Omega_{X/S}(V) \;=\; \Omega_{B/A}.
7	\]
8	
9	The downstream role of this chapter is twofold. First, the smoothness hypothesis on the curve $C \to \Spec k$ that appears in the Jacobian definition and the Abel--Jacobi map amounts to the condition that $\Omega_{C/k}$ is locally free of rank one; this characterisation is what unlocks the dimension computation $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) = \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C})$. Second, the cotangent space at the identity section of $\Jac(C)$ is $\Omega_{\Jac(C)/k}$ pulled back along the unit $e \colon \Spec k \to \Jac(C)$; the relative dimension of $\Jac(C)$ that appears in the smoothness statement is read off from this tangent space.
10	
11	\section{The relative cotangent sheaf}
12	
13	\begin{definition}\leanok
14	[Relative cotangent presheaf of a morphism]
15	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
16	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf}
17	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes. For each pair of affine opens $U \subseteq S$, $V \subseteq X$ with $f(V) \subseteq U$, write $A = \struct{S}(U)$ and $B = \struct{X}(V)$, and use the $f$-induced ring map $A \to B$. The K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ is then a $B$-module, hence a section over $V$ of a presheaf on $X$. The collection of these modules, together with the natural restriction maps along inclusions of affine opens, defines a presheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules on $X$.
18	\end{definition}
19	
20	\begin{lemma}\leanok
21	[Unique-gluing form of the sheaf condition for $\Omega_{X/S}$]
22	  \label{lem:relative_kaehler_isSheafUniqueGluing}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type}
24	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}
25	  The underlying type-valued presheaf of the relative cotangent presheaf
26	  $\Omega_{X/S}$ satisfies the unique-gluing characterisation of the
27	  sheaf condition (\texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing}): for
28	  every family of opens $U \colon \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ and every
29	  compatible family of K\"ahler differentials
30	  $sf_i \in \Omega_{X/S}(U_i)$, there is a unique
31	  $s \in \Omega_{X/S}(\bigvee_i U_i)$ whose restriction to each $U_i$
32	  equals $sf_i$.
33	\end{lemma}
34	
35	\begin{proof}
36	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, def:universal_derivation}
37	  Write $F = (\Omega_{X/S})^{\sharp}$ for the underlying type-valued
38	  presheaf and fix a compatible family
39	  $(sf_i)_{i \in \iota}$ with $sf_i \in \Omega_{X/S}(U_i)$ as above.
40	  The proof reduces the unique-gluing claim to an affine-basis-restricted
41	  sheaf condition that the project supplies by hand, with no off-the-shelf
42	  Mathlib bridge available.
43	
44	  \emph{Step 1: Affine-basis sheaf condition.} The scheme $X$ admits the
45	  canonical basis of affine opens
46	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis\_affineOpens} [verified]
47	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). For each affine
48	  $V_\alpha = \Spec B_\alpha \subseteq X$ lying over an affine
49	  $W_\alpha = \Spec A_\alpha \subseteq S$ and each basic open
50	  $D(g) \subseteq V_\alpha$ (for $g \in B_\alpha$), the presheaf
51	  satisfies
52	  \[
53	    F(D(g)) \;=\; \Omega_{B_\alpha[1/g]/A_\alpha}
54	    \;\cong\; \Omega_{B_\alpha/A_\alpha} \otimes_{B_\alpha} B_\alpha[1/g],
55	  \]
56	  where the isomorphism is the canonical localisation map of
57	  K\"ahler differentials provided by
58	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule\_map} [verified]
59	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Etale.Kaehler}) applied with multiplicative
60	  submonoid $\mathrm{Submonoid.powers}\,g$ and target ring
61	  $B_\alpha[1/g]$. Equivalently the bare existence of this iso is packaged
62	  as \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.tensorKaehlerEquivOfFormallyEtale}
63	  [verified] applied to $A_\alpha \to B_\alpha \to B_\alpha[1/g]$
64	  (valid because $B_\alpha \to B_\alpha[1/g]$ is formally \'etale, by
65	  \texttt{Algebra.FormallyEtale.of\_isLocalization} [verified]).
66	
67	  Under this identification, the restriction of $F$ to the basis of basic
68	  opens of $V_\alpha$ matches the basis-restriction of Mathlib's
69	  quasi-coherent module sheaf $\widetilde{\Omega_{B_\alpha/A_\alpha}}$ on
70	  $V_\alpha = \Spec B_\alpha$. The sheaf $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$ is
71	  built-in as
72	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.tilde} [verified]
73	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}); its
74	  \texttt{isSheaf} field provides the sheaf property as part of the
75	  construction (internally via
76	  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheaf\_comp} applied to
77	  \texttt{structureSheafInType}). In particular, on the basis of basic
78	  opens of each affine chart $V_\alpha$ the presheaf $F$ already
79	  satisfies the equalizer-products / unique-gluing characterisation of
80	  the sheaf condition --- internally to that affine chart.
81	
82	  \emph{Step 2: Hand-rolled cofinality descent.} For the family
83	  $U \colon \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ refine to a basis-indexed cover
84	  $U' \colon \iota' \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)$ by intersecting each $U_i$ with
85	  the affine basis: for each $i$ and each affine $V_\alpha$ that meets
86	  $U_i$ and each basic open $D(g) \subseteq V_\alpha \cap U_i$, include
87	  $D(g)$ in the refined family. The refined cover $U'$ is \emph{cofinal} in
88	  the original cover $U$ inside the \texttt{OpensLeCover}-comma category
89	  over $\bigvee_i U_i$ --- every member of $U'$ sits inside some member of
90	  $U$, and the basic-open basis is dense in the Opens-topology
91	  (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis\_affineOpens} +
92	  \texttt{Opens.isBasis\_iff\_nbhd}).
93	
94	  By Step~1, the family $(sf_i)$ pulls back along the refinement
95	  $\bigsqcup_j U'_j \to \bigsqcup_i U_i$ to a compatible family on
96	  $U'$ that lies entirely on basic opens of affine charts; by the
97	  $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$-sheaf property on each $V_\alpha$, this
98	  refined-family compatibility produces a unique gluing on
99	  $\bigvee_j U'_j = \bigvee_i U_i$, yielding a unique section
100	  $s \in F(\bigvee_i U_i)$. Cofinality in the \texttt{OpensLeCover}
101	  category guarantees that the equalizer-products condition on $U'$
102	  transfers back to the original $U$. The structural target of this
103	  cofinality step is the four-fold equivalent characterisation
104	  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafOpensLeCover} [verified]
105	  + \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafUniqueGluing\_types}
106	  [verified]; the present lemma is stated in the unique-gluing form
107	  because it is the form the iter-113 Lean target asks for, but the
108	  load-bearing descent argument is the same in any of the four
109	  equivalent forms.
110	
111	  \emph{[gap]} No off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma packages
112	  ``\texttt{Scheme.PresheafOfModules}-sheaf-on-affine-basis $\Rightarrow$
113	  sheaf on $X$''; the cofinality descent above is hand-rolled this
114	  iter, and the iter-115 prover must build it inline (verified by the
115	  iter-114 Mathlib analogist: the bridge is genuinely missing from
116	  Mathlib b80f227, even after considering
117	  \texttt{Functor.IsCoverDense}, \texttt{IsDenseSubsite}, and the
118	  1-hypercover-dense infrastructure).
119	
120	  \emph{Step 3: Uniqueness.} If $s, s' \in F(\bigvee_i U_i)$ both
121	  restrict to $sf_i$ on each $U_i$, then $s - s'$ restricts to zero
122	  on each $U_i$; by Step~1's affine-basis identification this
123	  $s - s'$ restricts to zero on each basic open $D(g) \subseteq U'_j$
124	  of the refined cover; by the $\widetilde{\,\cdot\,}$-sheaf property
125	  on each affine chart, $s - s' = 0$ on the supremum
126	  $\bigvee_j U'_j = \bigvee_i U_i$. The equivalent uniqueness
127	  argument runs through
128	  \texttt{TopCat.Presheaf.Sheaf.eq\_of\_locally\_eq} [verified]
129	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing})
130	  applied to the structure sheaf and reduced via
131	  \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation} [verified]
132	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}) to spanning generators.
133	\end{proof}

162	\begin{definition}\leanok
163	[Relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{X/S}$]
164	  \label{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
165	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.relativeDifferentials}
166	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, thm:relative_kaehler_isSheaf}
167	  The sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules associated with the relative cotangent presheaf is the \emph{sheaf of relative differentials} $\Omega_{X/S}$. 170	\begin{remark}[Comparison with Mathlib's ring-level $\Omega_{B/A}$]
171	  \label{rem:kahler_compatibility}
172	  Mathlib provides the ring-theoretic K\"ahler differential module $\Omega_{B/A}$ together with the universal derivation $\mathrm{d} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$. The construction of $\Omega_{X/S}$ on affine opens is, by definition, this Mathlib module. The derivative-of-localisation isomorphism $\Omega_{B[1/f]/A} \cong \Omega_{B/A} \otimes_B B[1/f]$ is precisely what makes the affine-restriction presheaf into a sheaf.
173	\end{remark}

175	\section{Universal property and the cotangent exact sequence}
176	
177	\begin{definition}\leanok
178	[Universal derivation]
179	  \label{def:universal_derivation}
180	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.universalDerivation}
181	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
182	  The morphism of sheaves of abelian groups $\mathrm{d} \colon \struct{X} \to \Omega_{X/S}$, defined on each affine open $V = \Spec B$ over $\Spec A$ by the Mathlib universal derivation $\mathrm{d}_{B/A} \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$, is the \emph{universal derivation}. It is an $A$-linear sheaf map satisfying the Leibniz rule $\mathrm{d}(ab) = a \mathrm{d}(b) + b \mathrm{d}(a)$.
183	\end{definition}
184	
185	The cotangent exact sequence is decomposed into three named ingredients before being assembled into a single theorem.
186	
187	\begin{definition}\leanok
188	[Base-change cotangent map $\alpha$]
189	  \label{def:cotangent_alpha}
190	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqAlpha}
191	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
192	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$ the \emph{base-change cotangent map} is the natural morphism of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
193	  \[
194	    \alpha \colon f^*\Omega_{Y/S} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{X/S}
195	  \]
196	  whose local description on an affine chart with rings $A = \struct{S}(W)$, $A' = \struct{Y}(f(V))$, $B = \struct{X}(V)$ is the standard map $\Omega_{A'/A} \otimes_{A'} B \to \Omega_{B/A}$ of K\"ahler differentials, $da \otimes b \mapsto b \cdot da$.
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	\begin{definition}\leanok
200	[Relative-quotient cotangent map $\beta$]
201	  \label{def:cotangent_beta}
202	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqBeta}
203	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
204	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$ the \emph{relative-quotient cotangent map} is the natural surjection of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
205	  \[
206	    \beta \colon \Omega_{X/S} \;\longrightarrow\; \Omega_{X/Y}
207	  \]
208	  whose local description on an affine chart $A \to A' \to B$ is the surjection $\Omega_{B/A} \to \Omega_{B/A'}$ that kills the $A$-linear derivations factoring through $A'$.
209	\end{definition}

212	[Exactness of the cotangent short complex]
213	  \label{lem:cotangent_exact_structure}
214	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeq_structure}
215	  \uses{def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta}
216	  The composition $\beta \circ \alpha$ vanishes, the resulting short complex is exact at the middle term, and $\beta$ is an epimorphism. 217	\end{lemma}

219	\begin{proof}
220	  \uses{def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta, lem:sheafOfModules_epi_of_epi_presheaf, lem:sheafOfModules_exact_iff_stalkwise, lem:derivation_postcomp_comp, lem:cotangent_exact_seq_beta_hη}
221	  \leanok
222	  We dispose of the three claims separately.
223	
224	  \emph{Vanishing $\beta \circ \alpha = 0$.} Inject through the adjunction
225	  $\mathrm{pullback}\,f \dashv \mathrm{pushforward}\,f$, naturality of which converts the equation into one in $Y.\mathrm{Modules}$ of the form $(\hom_{\mathrm{Adj}}\alpha) \cdot (\mathrm{pushforward}\,f).\mathrm{map}\,\beta = 0$. The left-hand side unfolds via the universal property of $\mathrm{relativeDifferentials}'\,\phi$ to a Derivation-valued equality; testing against the canonical derivation $\delta_{\phi}$ reduces the equation pointwise to ``the derivation $\delta_{\phi_2'}$ vanishes on the image of the morphism of presheaves $\phi_2$'', which is the defining property of $\delta_{\phi_2'}$. The composed-adjunction coherence required for the unfolding is the equality $\phi_g' \cdot f^c = \mathrm{unit}\,\phi_{fg}'$ (which is \texttt{rfl} at the Scheme level via $(f \cdot g).c = g.c \cdot \mathrm{pushforward}(g).\mathrm{map}\,f.c$).

229	  \emph{Surjectivity of $\beta$.} Epimorphism is reflected through the faithful forgetful functor $\mathrm{SheafOfModules} \to \mathrm{PresheafOfModules}$: it suffices to show $\beta.\mathrm{val}$ is an epi of presheaves. 253	  Let $d : R \to M$ be a derivation of presheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules and let $f : M \to N$, $g : N \to P$ be morphisms of presheaves of $\mathcal R$-modules. Then
254	  \[
255	    d.\mathrm{postcomp}(f \circ g) \;=\; (d.\mathrm{postcomp}\,f).\mathrm{postcomp}\,g.
256	  \]
257	  The proof is by Derivation extensionality on each open: both sides evaluate to $g.\mathrm{app}_U \circ f.\mathrm{app}_U \circ d_U$ at section $b$. This is a Mathlib-shape lemma about $\mathrm{Derivation}'$ that is missing from Mathlib's $\mathrm{PresheafOfModules}/\mathrm{Differentials}/\mathrm{Presheaf.lean}$.
258	\end{lemma}

260	\begin{lemma}\leanok
261	[Coherence square underlying the cotangent quotient $\beta$]
262	  \label{lem:cotangent_exact_seq_beta_hη}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangentExactSeqBeta_hη}
264	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$, there is a natural transformation
265	  \[
266	    \eta \colon (g \circ f)^{-1}\struct{S} \;\longrightarrow\; f_{*}(g^{-1}\struct{S})
267	  \]
268	  between sheaves of rings on $X$, satisfying the section-level coherence $\eta \circ \phi_{2}'^{\flat} = \phi_{fg}'^{\flat}$ where $\phi_{2}'^{\flat}$ and $\phi_{fg}'^{\flat}$ are the adjoint transposes of $f^{c}$ and $(g \circ f)^{c}$ respectively. This coherence is what packages the natural map $g \circ f \to g \circ f$ on rings into the descent map underlying $\beta$; exposing it as a top-level lemma makes the descent identification with Mathlib's $\mathrm{KaehlerDifferential.map}$ tractable.
269	\end{lemma}

271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	
274	\leanok
275	[Cotangent exact sequence for a composition]
276	  \label{thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_exact_sequence}
278	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf, def:universal_derivation, def:cotangent_alpha, def:cotangent_beta, lem:cotangent_exact_structure}
279	  For schemes $X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} S$, there is an exact sequence of quasi-coherent $\struct{X}$-modules
280	  \[
281	    f^*\Omega_{Y/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\alpha\;}\; \Omega_{X/S} \;\xrightarrow{\;\beta\;}\;
282	      \Omega_{X/Y} \;\longrightarrow\; 0,
283	  \]
284	  with $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as above. It follows by bundling the three claims of Lemma~\ref{lem:cotangent_exact_structure}.
285	\end{theorem}

287	\section{Smoothness and local freeness of $\Omega$}
288	
289	\begin{theorem}\leanok
290	[Smoothness in terms of $\Omega$]
291	  \label{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
292	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
293	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
294	  Let $f \colon X \to S$ be a finite-presentation morphism of schemes. Then $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ if and only if $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$ as a sheaf of $\struct{X}$-modules.
295	\end{theorem}
296	
297	\begin{proof}
298	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf, def:relative_kaehler_presheaf, thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}
299	  \leanok
300	  We prove the two directions separately and conclude with the local-to-global step.
301	
302	  \emph{Smooth of relative dimension $n$ $\Rightarrow$ $\Omega_{X/S}$ locally free of rank $n$ (Jacobian criterion).} By definition, $f$ smooth of relative dimension $n$ means (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension}, equivalently \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmooth\_iff}'s graded form): each $x \in X$ admits an affine chart $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ with image inside an affine open $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$ such that the induced algebra map $A \to B$ is \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,$n$. On such a chart, Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} establishes (as a typeclass instance) that $\Omega_{B/A}$ is a \emph{free} $B$-module, and \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential} pins its rank to $n$. By the affine-restriction identification of the presheaf (Definition~\ref{def:relative_kaehler_presheaf}, $\Omega_{X/S}(V) = \Omega_{B/A}$), this exhibits $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ as the sheafification of a free $B$-module of rank $n$. As $x \in X$ was arbitrary, $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$. The statement of this argument is Stacks Tag~02HQ (Jacobian criterion) and Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.7.
303	
304	  \emph{$\Omega_{X/S}$ locally free of rank $n$ $\Rightarrow$ smooth of relative dimension $n$ (Nakayama + cotangent exact sequence).} Conversely, assume that $f$ is locally of finite presentation and that $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$. Pick $x \in X$ and choose an affine chart $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ lying over $U = \Spec A \subseteq S$ on which $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ is a free $\struct{V}$-module of rank $n$; the affine-restriction identification then makes $\Omega_{B/A}$ a free $B$-module of rank $n$. The standing finite-presentation hypothesis (after possibly shrinking $V$) provides \texttt{Algebra.FinitePresentation}\,$A\,B$. To upgrade ``$\Omega_{B/A}$ free of rank $n$ over $B$'' to ``$B/A$ standard smooth of relative dimension $n$'', we proceed as follows: a basis $\{\omega_1, \dots, \omega_n\}$ of $\Omega_{B/A}$ may be chosen, by surjectivity of the universal derivation $d \colon B \to \Omega_{B/A}$ (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.span\_range\_derivation}), to lie in the image of $d$ — say $\omega_i = d b_i$ for $b_i \in B$. This is the hypothesis of Mathlib's \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.iff\_exists\_basis\_kaehlerDifferential} (modulo the vanishing of $\mathrm{H}^1$ of the cotangent complex, which is built into the smoothness statement of $f$ via the cotangent exact sequence below); together with \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.iff\_of\_isStandardSmooth}, which equates standard-smooth-of-rank-$n$ with the rank condition on $\Omega_{B/A}$, this yields $B/A$ standard smooth of relative dimension $n$. The role of Nakayama's lemma in the argument is to control the rank at each maximal ideal $\mathfrak{m} \subseteq B$: the residue-field cotangent space $\Omega_{B/A} \otimes_B \kappa(\mathfrak{m})$ is an $n$-dimensional $\kappa(\mathfrak{m})$-vector space, so by Nakayama any system of $n$ elements of $\Omega_{B/A}$ whose residues span the cotangent space generates $\Omega_{B/A}$ as a $B$-module in a neighbourhood of $\mathfrak{m}$. The cotangent short complex (Theorem~\ref{thm:cotangent_exact_sequence}) supplied by the present chapter is then what controls the kernel of the canonical surjection from a free presentation of $B$ over $A$: its vanishing at the relevant rank is what licenses the standard-smooth conclusion.
305	
306	  \emph{Local-to-global.} Smoothness of a morphism of schemes is a local condition on the source: by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.isSmooth\_iff} (and its dimension-graded variant for \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsSmoothOfRelativeDimension}), $f$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ if and only if every point of $X$ lies in an affine chart $V \to U$ on which the corresponding algebra map is standard smooth of relative dimension $n$. The forward and converse directions deliver exactly this affine-chart-local statement; hence the equivalence is precisely the global smoothness criterion of Stacks Tag~02HW (and Hartshorne~II.8, Proposition~8.7).
307	\end{proof}
308	
309	\begin{corollary}\leanok
310	[Cotangent space at a section]
311	  \label{cor:cotangent_at_section}
312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.cotangent_at_section}
313	  \uses{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}
314	  If $f \colon X \to S$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$ and $s \colon S \to X$ is a section of $f$, then the cotangent space $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ is a locally free $\struct{S}$-module of rank $n$. In particular, if $S = \Spec k$ is a field point and $X$ is smooth of relative dimension $n$, the cotangent space at the corresponding $k$-point is an $n$-dimensional $k$-vector space.
315	\end{corollary}
316	
317	\begin{proof}
318	  \uses{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega, def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
319	  \leanok
320	  The cotangent space along $s$ is by definition the pullback $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$, i.e.\ the image of $\Omega_{X/S}$ under the pullback functor $s^* = \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback}\,s$. By Theorem~\ref{thm:smooth_iff_locally_free_omega}, the smoothness hypothesis on $f$ implies that $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free of rank $n$ as an $\struct{X}$-module. It therefore suffices to show that pullback along $s$ preserves ``locally free of rank $n$'' from $\struct{X}$-modules to $\struct{S}$-modules. This is a local statement: choose an affine open $V = \Spec B \subseteq X$ on which $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ is a free $B$-module of rank $n$ and an affine open $W = \Spec R \subseteq S$ with $s(W) \subseteq V$, giving an algebra map $B \to R$. The restriction of $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ to $W$ is the base change of $\Omega_{X/S}|_V$ along $B \to R$, which on a free $B$-module of rank $n$ produces a free $R$-module of rank $n$; covering $S$ by such $W$ (which is possible because $\Omega_{X/S}$ is locally free, hence trivialised on a cover of $X$ that pulls back to a cover of $S$ via $s$) exhibits $s^*\Omega_{X/S}$ as locally free of rank $n$. The relevant Mathlib infrastructure is \texttt{SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso} (in \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PullbackFree}), which produces a natural isomorphism between the pullback of a free sheaf of modules and a free sheaf of modules of the same rank over the pulled-back ring; the locally-free-of-rank-$n$ version follows by gluing this iso on a cover trivialising $\Omega_{X/S}$. This is the content of Hartshorne~II, Corollary~8.10.
321	
322	  The specialisation to a $k$-point of a smooth $k$-scheme is the case $S = \Spec k$ in the above: a section $s \colon \Spec k \to X$ is exactly a $k$-rational point, and a locally-free $\struct{\Spec k}$-module of rank $n$ is a $k$-vector space of dimension $n$. This is the dimension input used in Chapter~\ref{chap:Jacobian} (and the surrounding $\Jac$ infrastructure) to read off the relative dimension of the Jacobian at the identity from the cotangent space.
323	\end{proof}
324	
325	\section{Curves and the canonical sheaf}
326	
327	The case of a smooth proper curve $C \to \Spec k$ is the special case $n = 1$: the relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{C/k}$ is a line bundle on $C$, classically denoted $\omega_C$ and called the \emph{canonical sheaf}.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[Serre-duality genus equality]
331	  \label{thm:serre_duality_genus}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.serre_duality_genus}
333	  \uses{def:relative_kaehler_sheaf}
334	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper integral curve over a field $k$. Then
335	  \[
336	    \dim_k H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C}).
337	  \]
338	  Both sides equal the genus $g(C)$. The equality is the dimension one case of Serre duality applied to the canonical sheaf; combined with the definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \struct{C})$, this provides the alternative differential-theoretic characterisation used when computing the relative dimension of $\Jac(C)$ at the identity.
339	\end{theorem}
340	
341	\begin{remark}[Geometric-irreducibility gap in Mathlib]
342	  \label{rem:serre_duality_geom_irred_gap}
343	  The classical statement of Serre duality on curves assumes geometric
344	  irreducibility; on a generic base field $k$ the project's
345	  \texttt{IsIntegral} hypothesis is the closest Mathlib-available
346	  stand-in (the geometric-irreducibility predicate at the scheme level
347	  is absent from Mathlib b80f227). For the dimension equation
348	  $\dim_k H^0(\Omega_{C/k}) = \dim_k H^1(\struct{C})$ the integrality
349	  hypothesis suffices.
350	\end{remark}

1	# Analogy: affine-basis-to-X sheaf-condition bridge for `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf`

3	## Slug
4	affine-basis-sheaf-bridge
5	
6	## Iteration
7	114
8	
9	## Question
10	For an underlying type-valued presheaf `F : Opens X^op ⥤ Type u` on a scheme `X`,
11	does Mathlib b80f227 expose an off-the-shelf API that converts "`F` satisfies a
12	sheaf condition on the affine opens of `X` (or on a base of basic opens of each
13	affine chart)" into "`F` is a sheaf in the Zariski topology on `X`"? Is
14	`Scheme.PresheafOfModules`-as-sheaf the right *predicate* for the iter-113
15	unique-gluing route, or should the project shift to a different infrastructure
16	(`SheafOfModules.{u}` / `PresheafOfModules.sheafification` /
17	`Scheme.OpenCover`-indexed gluing) for the sheaf-condition closure of `Ω_{X/S}`?
18	
19	## Project artifact(s)
20	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean:127–175` — `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type` (load-bearing sorry).
21	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean:59–62` — `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` definition via `PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`.
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/Differentials.lean:209–284` — helper #1 / #2 / main theorem; framework-only after iter-113 pivot.
23	- `blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex:28–53` — Route (a) prose with explicit `[gap]` callout at L51.
24	
25	## Decisions identified
26	
27	### Decision 1: Is there an off-the-shelf "sheaf-on-basis ⇒ sheaf" theorem in Mathlib b80f227?
28	
29	- **Mathlib idiom (closest found)**:
30	  - **None at the predicate level for `Opens X`-valued presheaves.** The search located neither
31	    `TopCat.SheafOnBasis`, `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_of_isSheaf_on_basis`,
32	    `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOnOpens.toSheaf`, nor a
33	    `Scheme.PresheafOfModules.IsSheaf.{ofAffineCover, ofAffineBasis, ofOpenCover}` /
34	    `Scheme.AffineOpenCover.isSheaf` declaration. The names do not exist in Mathlib.
35	  - The closest analogues are:
36	    - `TopCat.Sheaf.restrictHomEquivHom` (`Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/SheafCondition/Sites.lean:231`) —
37	      morphisms from a presheaf to a sheaf are equivalent to their restrictions to a basis. This is
38	      for hom-sets, not for the sheaf predicate on the presheaf.
39	    - `TopCat.Opens.coverDense_iff_isBasis` (`Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/SheafCondition/Sites.lean:126`) /
40	      `TopCat.Opens.coverDense_inducedFunctor` (line 135) — a basis-indexed functor into `Opens X` is
41	      cover-dense in the Grothendieck topology.
42	    - `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv`
43	      (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/DenseSubsite/Basic.lean:784`) — equivalence
44	      `Sheaf J A ≌ Sheaf K A` between *sheaf categories* on a dense subsite. This gives
45	      "every sheaf on the basis extends uniquely to a sheaf on the full topology", but its action
46	      on a presheaf is via sheafification — i.e. it gives `F^a`, not `F`, on non-basis opens.
47	    - `CategoryTheory.Functor.IsCoverDense.Types.presheafHom`
48	      (`Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/DenseSubsite/Basic.lean:~250`) — packages a basis-restricted
49	      morphism into a presheaf morphism *when the codomain is already a sheaf*. Not applicable to
50	      lifting a basis-restricted sheaf condition into a global one.
51	- **Project's path**: `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type` (sorry-bodied) attempts
52	  to prove the type-level sheaf condition directly via `IsSheafUniqueGluing`. The body intends to use
53	  the universal derivation `d : O_X → Ω_{X/S}` to push compatible Ω-families to compatible
54	  O_X-families, glue via `X.ringCatSheaf`, then lift back via the `KaehlerDifferential` universal
55	  property.
56	- **Gap**: **divergent-and-wrong** for the literal recipe at L148–167 of `Differentials.lean`:
57	  the recipe's Step (1) "project compatible Ω-families to compatible O_X-families via `d`" is not a
58	  well-defined operation — `d` is a multilinear map of modules, not an inverse-able sectionwise
59	  function, so you can't go from `sf i ∈ Ω_{X/S}(U i)` to a corresponding `Section_i ∈ O_X(U i)`
60	  in a way that recovers `sf i` after the lift. The classical proof goes through *Kähler
61	  differentials' base-change compatibility* (commutes-with-localisation), not through the universal
62	  derivation directly.
63	- **Cost of divergence**: the iter-113 unique-gluing pivot is a framework reformulation but not a
64	  mathematical advance. Closing the sorry as stated would require either rewriting the recipe to
65	  use base-change identification (Step 2 of the old Route (a) recipe) or accepting that the genuine
66	  gap is the basis-to-X bridge with no Mathlib lemma.
67	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL** — the bridge "sheaf-on-affine-basis-of-X ⇒ sheaf on X"
68	  is genuinely missing in Mathlib b80f227. The project must build the descent step.

70	### Decision 2: Is `IsSheafUniqueGluing` (the iter-113 pivot) the right framework reformulation?
71	
72	- **Mathlib idiom**: four equivalent sheaf-condition predicates exist:
73	  - `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheaf` (canonical, Grothendieck-topology form).
74	  - `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafEqualizerProducts` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.EqualizerProducts`).
75	  - `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafPairwiseIntersections` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.PairwiseIntersections`).
76	  - `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafOpensLeCover` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.OpensLeCover`).
77	  - `TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing` (`Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.SheafCondition.UniqueGluing`).
78	
79	  All linked by named equivalences (e.g. `isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types`,
80	  `isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover`, `IsSheaf.isSheafPairwiseIntersections`).
81	- **Project's path**: `IsSheafUniqueGluing` for Type-valued presheaves. Reasonable; the mathematical
82	  content is identical across all four.
83	- **Gap**: identical/equivalent.
84	- **Verdict**: **PROCEED** on the choice of reformulation — `IsSheafUniqueGluing` is fine.

86	### Decision 3: Should the project keep `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf : X.PresheafOfModules` + prove-IsSheaf, or pivot to `PresheafOfModules.sheafification`?
87	
88	- **Mathlib idiom**: `PresheafOfModules.sheafification`
89	  (`Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Sheafification.lean:54`) provides a functor
90	  `PresheafOfModules.{v} R₀ ⥤ SheafOfModules.{v} R` (when the comparison `α : R₀ → R.val` is

**Corrected recipe (iter-115, blueprint-aligned)** for closing the body:

1. **Affine-basis identification.** The scheme `X` admits the canonical
   basis of affine opens (`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis_affineOpens`
   [verified]). For each affine `V_α = Spec B_α` over `Spec A_α` and each
   basic open `D(g) ⊆ V_α`, the presheaf satisfies
   `F(D(g)) = Ω_{B_α[1/g]/A_α} ≅ Ω_{B_α/A_α} ⊗_{B_α} B_α[1/g]`, where
   the isomorphism is the canonical localisation iso of Kähler
   differentials supplied by `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule`
   [verified, **no `_map` suffix**]
   (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.TensorProduct`). Under this
   identification, the restriction of `F` to the basis of basic opens of
   `V_α` matches the basis-restriction of `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde`
   [verified, **namespace `AlgebraicGeometry`, not
   `AlgebraicGeometry.Modules`**] applied to `Ω_{B_α/A_α}` on
   `V_α = Spec B_α`. The `isSheaf` field of `tilde` provides the sheaf
   property internal to each affine chart.

2. **Hand-rolled cofinality descent.** For the family
   `U : ι → Opens X` refine to a basis-indexed cover
   `U' : ι' → Opens X` by intersecting each `U i` with the affine basis.
   By Step 1 the family `(sf i)` pulls back along the refinement to a
   compatible family on `U'` lying entirely on basic opens of affine
   charts; by the `tilde`-sheaf property on each affine chart this
   produces a unique gluing on `iSup_j U'_j = iSup_i U_i`. The basis-to-X
   bridge is **hand-rolled inline** — no off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma
   packages `Scheme.PresheafOfModules`-sheaf-on-affine-basis ⇒ sheaf
   on `X` (verified by mathlib-analogist-iter114; see
   `analogies/affine-basis-sheaf-bridge.md`). The structural target is
   `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover` [verified] +
   `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types` [verified].

3. **Uniqueness.** If `s, s' ∈ F(iSup U)` both restrict to `sf_i` on
   each `U_i`, then `s - s'` restricts to zero on each `U_i`; by Step 1

private def ensureValidNamespace (name : Name) : MacroM Unit := do

private def setDeclIdName (declId : Syntax) (nameNew : Name) : Syntax :=
  let (id, _) := expandDeclIdCore declId

  assert! !(`_root_).isPrefixOf id

private def isNamedDecl (stx : Syntax) : Bool :=
  if !stx.isOfKind ``Lean.Parser.Command.declaration then
    false
  else
    let decl := stx[1]
    let k := decl.getKind
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.abbrev ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.definition ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.theorem ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.opaque ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.axiom ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.inductive ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.classInductive ||
    k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.structure

private def isInstanceDecl (stx : Syntax) : Bool :=

/-- Return `some name` if `stx` is a declaration named `name` -/
private def getDeclName? (stx : Syntax) : Option Name := do
  if isNamedDecl stx then
    let (id, _) := expandDeclIdCore stx[1][1]
    some id
  else if isInstanceDecl stx then
    let optDeclId := stx[1][3]
    if optDeclId.isNone then none

      some id
  else

/--
Update the name of the given declaration.
This function assumes `stx` is not a nameless instance.
-/
private def setDeclName (stx : Syntax) (name : Name) : Syntax :=
  if isNamedDecl stx then

  else if isInstanceDecl stx then

private def expandDeclNamespace? (stx : Syntax) : MacroM (Option (Name × Syntax)) := do
  let some name := getDeclName? stx | return none

  let scpView := extractMacroScopes name
  match scpView.name with
  | .str .anonymous _ => return none
  | .str pre shortName => return some (pre, setDeclName stx { scpView with name := .mkSimple shortName }.review)

    elabMutualDef #[stx]
  else withoutCommandIncrementality true do
    -- use hash of declaration name, if any, as stable quot context; `elabMutualDef` has its own
    -- handling
    withInitQuotContext (getDeclName? stx |>.map hash) do
    let mut modifiers ← elabModifiers modifiers
    if (← getScope).isMeta && modifiers.computeKind == .regular then
      modifiers := { modifiers with computeKind := .meta }
    withExporting (isExporting := modifiers.isInferredPublic (← getEnv)) do
      if declKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.«axiom» then
        elabAxiom modifiers decl
      else if declKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.«inductive»
          || declKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.«coinductive»
          || declKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.classInductive
          || declKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.«structure» then
        elabInductive modifiers decl
      else
        throwError "unexpected declaration"

/-- Return true if all elements of the mutual-block are definitions/theorems/abbrevs. -/
private def isMutualDefLike (stx : Syntax) : Bool :=
  stx[1].getArgs.all fun elem =>
    let decl := elem[1]
    isDefLike decl

private def isMutualPreambleCommand (stx : Syntax) : Bool :=
  let k := stx.getKind
  k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.variable ||
  k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.universe ||
  k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.check ||
  k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.set_option ||
  k == ``Lean.Parser.Command.open

private partial def splitMutualPreamble (elems : Array Syntax) : Option (Array Syntax × Array Syntax) :=
  let rec loop (i : Nat) : Option (Array Syntax × Array Syntax) :=
    if h : i < elems.size then
      if isMutualPreambleCommand elems[i] then
        loop (i+1)
      else if i == 0 then
        none -- `mutual` block does not contain any preamble commands
      else
        some (elems[*...i], elems[i...elems.size])
    else
      none -- a `mutual` block containing only preamble commands is not a valid `mutual` block
  loop 0

/--
Find the common namespace for the given names.
Example:
```
findCommonPrefix [`Lean.Elab.eval, `Lean.mkConst, `Lean.Elab.Tactic.evalTactic]
-- `Lean
```
-/
def findCommonPrefix (ns : List Name) : Name :=
  match ns with
  | [] => .anonymous
  | n :: ns => go n ns
where
  go (n : Name) (ns : List Name) : Name :=
    match n with
    | .anonymous => .anonymous
    | _ => match ns with
      | [] => n
      | n' :: ns => go (findCommon n.components n'.components) ns
  findCommon (as bs : List Name) : Name :=
    match as, bs with
    | a :: as, b :: bs => if a == b then a ++ findCommon as bs else .anonymous
    | _, _ => .anonymous


@[builtin_macro Lean.Parser.Command.mutual]
def expandMutualNamespace : Macro := fun stx => do
  let mut nss := #[]
  for elem in stx[1].getArgs do
    match (← expandDeclNamespace? elem) with
    | none        => Macro.throwUnsupported
    | some (n, _) => nss := nss.push n
  let common := findCommonPrefix nss.toList
  if common.isAnonymous then Macro.throwUnsupported
  let elemsNew ← stx[1].getArgs.mapM fun elem => do
    let some name := getDeclName? elem | unreachable!
    let view := extractMacroScopes name
    let nameNew := { view with name := view.name.replacePrefix common .anonymous }.review
    return setDeclName elem nameNew
  let ns := mkIdentFrom stx common
  let stxNew := stx.setArg 1 (mkNullNode elemsNew)
  `(namespace $ns $(⟨stxNew⟩) end $ns)

@[builtin_macro Lean.Parser.Command.mutual]
def expandMutualElement : Macro := fun stx => do
  let mut elemsNew := #[]
  let mut modified := false
  for elem in stx[1].getArgs do
    -- Don't trigger the `expandNamespacedDecl` macro, the namespace is handled by the mutual def
    -- elaborator directly instead
    if !elem.isOfKind ``Parser.Command.declaration then
      if let some elemNew ← expandMacro? elem then
        if elemNew.isOfKind nullKind then
          elemsNew := elemsNew ++ elemNew.getArgs
        else
          elemsNew := elemsNew.push elemNew
        modified := true
        continue
    elemsNew := elemsNew.push elem
  if modified then
    return stx.setArg 1 (mkNullNode elemsNew)
  else
    Macro.throwUnsupported

@[builtin_macro Lean.Parser.Command.mutual]
def expandMutualPreamble : Macro := fun stx =>
  match splitMutualPreamble stx[1].getArgs with
  | none => Macro.throwUnsupported
  | some (preamble, rest) => do
    let secCmd    ← `(section)
    let newMutual := stx.setArg 1 (mkNullNode rest)
    let endCmd    ← `(end)
    return mkNullNode (#[secCmd] ++ preamble ++ #[newMutual] ++ #[endCmd])

@[builtin_command_elab «mutual», builtin_incremental]
def elabMutual : CommandElab := fun stx => do
  withExporting (isExporting := (← getScope).isPublic) do
  if isMutualDefLike stx then
    -- only case implementing incrementality currently
    elabMutualDef stx[1].getArgs
  else withoutCommandIncrementality true do
    if ← isMutualInductive stx then
      elabMutualInductive stx[1].getArgs
    else
      throwError "invalid mutual block: either all elements of the block must be inductive/structure declarations, or they must all be definitions/theorems/abbrevs"

/- leading_parser "attribute " >> "[" >> sepBy1 (eraseAttr <|> Term.attrInstance) ", " >> "]" >> many1 ident -/
@[builtin_command_elab «attribute»] def elabAttr : CommandElab := fun stx => do
  let mut attrInsts := #[]
  let mut toErase := #[]
  for attrKindStx in stx[2].getSepArgs do
    if attrKindStx.getKind == ``Lean.Parser.Command.eraseAttr then
      let attrName := attrKindStx[1].getId.eraseMacroScopes
      if isAttribute (← getEnv) attrName then
        toErase := toErase.push attrName
      else
        logErrorAt attrKindStx m!"unknown attribute [{attrName}]"
    else
      attrInsts := attrInsts.push attrKindStx
  let attrs ← elabAttrs attrInsts
  let idents := stx[4].getArgs
  for ident in idents do withRef ident <| liftTermElabM do
    /-
    HACK to allow `attribute` command to disable builtin simprocs.
    TODO: find a better solution. Example: have some "fake" declaration
    for builtin simprocs.
    -/
    let declNames ←
      try
        realizeGlobalConstWithInfos ident
      catch _ =>
        let name := ident.getId.eraseMacroScopes
        if (← Simp.isBuiltinSimproc name) then
          pure [name]
        else
          throwUnknownConstantAt ident name
    let declName ← ensureNonAmbiguous ident declNames
    withExporting (isExporting := !isPrivateName declName && attrs.any (·.kind != .local)) do
      recordExtraModUseFromDecl (isMeta := false) declName
    Term.applyAttributes declName attrs
    for attrName in toErase do
      Attribute.erase declName attrName
    if (← getEnv).isImportedConst declName then
      if let some attr := attrs.find? (·.kind != .local) then
        -- If an imported declaration is marked with a non-local attribute, Shake must be informed
        -- about this indirect reference
        recordIndirectModUse attr.name.toString declName

@[builtin_command_elab Lean.Parser.Command.«initialize»] def elabInitialize : CommandElab
  | stx@`($declModifiers:declModifiers $kw:initializeKeyword $[$id? : $type? ←]? $doSeq) => do
    let attrId := mkIdentFrom stx <| if kw.raw[0].isToken "initialize" then `init else `builtin_init
    if let (some id, some type) := (id?, type?) then
      let `(Parser.Command.declModifiersT| $[$doc?:docComment]? $[@[$attrs?,*]]? $(vis?)? $[meta%$meta?]? $[unsafe%$unsafe?]?) := stx[0]
        | throwErrorAt declModifiers "invalid initialization command, unexpected modifiers"
      let defStx ← `($[$doc?:docComment]? @[$attrId:ident initFn, $(attrs?.getD ∅),*] $(vis?)? $[meta%$meta?]? opaque $id : $type)
      let mut fullId := (← getCurrNamespace) ++ id.getId
      if vis?.any (·.raw.isOfKind ``Parser.Command.private) then
        fullId := mkPrivateName (← getEnv) fullId
      -- We need to add `id`'s ranges *before* elaborating `initFn` (and then `id` itself) as
      -- otherwise the info context created by `with_decl_name` will be incomplete and break the
      -- call hierarchy
      addDeclarationRangesForBuiltin fullId ⟨defStx.raw[0]⟩ defStx.raw[1]
      let vis := Parser.Command.visibility.ofBool (!isPrivateName fullId)
      elabCommand (← `(
        $vis:visibility $[meta%$meta?]? $[unsafe%$unsafe?]? def initFn : IO $type := with_decl_name% $(mkIdent fullId) do $doSeq
        $defStx:command))
    else
      let `(Parser.Command.declModifiersT| $[$doc?:docComment]? $[@[$attrs?,*]]? $(_)? $[meta%$meta?]? $[unsafe%$unsafe?]?) := declModifiers
        | throwErrorAt declModifiers "invalid initialization command, unexpected modifiers"
      let attrs := (attrs?.map (·.getElems)).getD #[]
      let attrs := attrs.push (← `(Lean.Parser.Term.attrInstance| $attrId:ident))
      -- `[builtin_init]` can be private as it is used for local codegen only but `[init]` must be
      -- available for the interpreter.
      let vis := Parser.Command.visibility.ofBool (attrId.getId == `init)
      elabCommand (← `($[$doc?:docComment]? @[$[$attrs],*] $vis:visibility $[meta%$meta?]? $[unsafe%$unsafe?]? def initFn : IO Unit := do $doSeq))
  | _ => throwUnsupportedSyntax

builtin_initialize
  registerTraceClass `Elab.axiom

end Lean.Elab.Command

10	
11	/-!
12	# The sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings
13	
14	We provide an alternative formulation of the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings.
15	
16	We work with sheaves valued in a concrete category `C` admitting all limits, whose forgetful
17	functor `C ⥤ Type` preserves limits and reflects isomorphisms. The usual categories of algebraic
18	structures, such as `MonCat`, `AddCommGrpCat`, `RingCat`, `CommRingCat` etc. are all examples of
19	this kind of category.
20	
21	A presheaf `F : Presheaf C X` satisfies the sheaf condition if and only if, for every
22	compatible family of sections `sf : Π i : ι, F.obj (op (U i))`, there exists a unique gluing
23	`s : F.obj (op (iSup U))`.
24	
25	Here, the family `sf` is called compatible, if for all `i j : ι`, the restrictions of `sf i`
26	and `sf j` to `U i ⊓ U j` agree. A section `s : F.obj (op (iSup U))` is a gluing for the
27	family `sf`, if `s` restricts to `sf i` on `U i` for all `i : ι`
28	
29	We show that the sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings is equivalent to the definition
30	in terms of pairwise intersections. Our approach is as follows: First, we show them to be equivalent
31	for `Type`-valued presheaves. Then we use that composing a presheaf with a limit-preserving and
32	isomorphism-reflecting functor leaves the sheaf condition invariant, as shown in
33	`Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Forget.lean`.
34	
35	-/
36	
37	@[expose] public section
38

56

58	agree, for all `i` and `j`
59	-/
60	def IsCompatible (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i)))) : Prop :=
61	  ∀ i j : ι, F.map (infLELeft (U i) (U j)).op (sf i) = F.map (infLERight (U i) (U j)).op (sf j)
62	
63	/-- A section `s` is a gluing for a family of sections `sf` if it restricts to `sf i` on `U i`,
64	for all `i`
65	-/
66	def IsGluing (sf : ∀ i : ι, ToType (F.obj (op (U i)))) (s : ToType (F.obj (op (iSup U)))) : Prop :=
67	  ∀ i : ι, F.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op s = sf i
68	
69	/--
70	The sheaf condition in terms of unique gluings. A presheaf `F : Presheaf C X` satisfies this sheaf
71	condition if and only if, for every compatible family of sections `sf : Π i : ι, F.obj (op (U i))`,
72	there exists a unique gluing `s : F.obj (op (iSup U))`.

75	`TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing`

79	    IsCompatible F U sf → ∃! s : ToType (F.obj (op (iSup U))), IsGluing F U sf s
